evidence: Developing our own AI technologies requires resources to develop, test, and maintain such technologies, which could be costly.
We use AI technologies in some of our business processes, including some consumer-facing features.
We use AI technologies in some of our business processes, including some consumer-facing features.
Adding AI technologies, especially generative AI, in both new and existing business processes may introduce additional risks.
Threat actors are increasingly leveraging AI to enhance the sophistication, speed, and effectiveness of cyberattacks. The proliferation of AI tools may lower barriers for threat actors.
Using AI technologies may lead to accuracy issues, security vulnerabilities, or biases, which may compromise data security, intellectual property, or client information.
We may also fail to adopt AI technologies at an appropriate pace, which could put us at a competitive disadvantage.

limitations: No quantified AI spend, headcount, or capex figures appear in the excerpts; investment intensity scored 3 based on named initiatives and internal development references only.
AI use cases named (consumer-facing features, business processes, generative AI) but no deployment metrics or quantified outcomes disclosed; concreteness capped at 3.

Risk disclosures describe specific mechanisms (cybersecurity amplification, accuracy/bias issues, competitive disadvantage from under-adoption) but include no quantified risk figures or named governance/oversight bodies, capping risk depth at 4.
